Klasse AdvancedExamples

java.lang.Object
de.btu.kt.syx.tutorials.AdvancedExamples
public class AdvancedExamples extends Object
Coding examples of handling bulk data.

  • Methodendetails

    • addParamInfo

      public static void addParamInfo()
      Demonstrates how to add parameter information.

      [<prev] | Back to [Advanced Examples] [Tutorials] | [next>]

      Adding Parameter Information

      In most cases, the "MIDI value" of a parameter as stored in the SysEx message does not equal the respective "model value" as displayed on the device. There are also cases where not every MIDI value which can potentially be stored in the respective parameter bits of the SysEx message are permissible. For code clarity (and also for parameter validation) it is desirable to work with the model values rather than with the MIDI values. To this end, SYX features parameter information.

      We will enhance the last example by adding parameter information to the received error messages. We use the same Java code and just modify the receiveSysexMsg(…) method of the SysEx message listener:

        @Override
  public void receiveSysexMsg(SysexMessage sxMsg, long timeStamp)
  throws InvalidMidiDataException
  {
    SyxDataStruct prt;
    SyxParamInfo  pi;

      If we use parameter information, each parameter in the SysEx message needs a unique identifier (UID). Therefore, we define such UIDs for the two parameters, device number and error number:

          final String UID_DEVICE_NUM = "DEVICE_NUM";
    final String UID_ERROR_MSG  = "ERROR_MSG";

      First, we create one message part for the content of the error message:

          final String errMsgFormat = "43H 1#H 35H 7FH 00H[4] eeH";
    prt = new SyxDataStruct(errMsgFormat,"Error Information");

      Now we add parameter information to the part. We start with the device number '#'. The MIDI value is in the range [ 0x00, 0x0F ]. The model is offset by one and takes values in the range [ 1, 16] The respective parameter information is:

          pi = new SyxParamInfo(prt,'#',UID_DEVICE_NUM,"Device");
    pi.setValueRange(0x00,0x0F,1);

      Don't forget to add the parameter information to the part:

          prt.addParamInfo(pi);

      Next we add information for the error number parameter 'e'. It has 7 bits, i.e., a potential MIDI value range of [ 0x00, 0x7F ]. Actually, its permissible MIDI values are in the range [ 0x01, 0x20 ] (see [1], pp. Add-16,17). Further, the parameter has plain-text model values. Hence, the parameter information is:

          int[]    errVals = SYX.getIntRange(0x01,0x20);
    String[] errMsgs = new String[]
    {
      "MIDI Buffer Full" /*0x01*/, "SEQ Buffer Full"  /*0x02*/,
      "MIDI Data"        /*0x03*/, "MIDI Check Sum"   /*0x04*/,
      "MIDI Device Off"  /*0x05*/, "MIDI Bulk Prot."  /*0x06*/,
      "No Data Card"     /*0x07*/, "Data Card Prot."  /*0x08*/,
      "Data Format"      /*0x09*/, "Illegal Data"     /*0x0A*/,
      "Verify Failed"    /*0x0B*/, "Internal Bat.Lo"  /*0x0C*/,
      "Data Card Bat.Lo" /*0x0D*/, "SEQ Memory Full"  /*0x0E*/,
      "SEQ Data Empty"   /*0x0F*/, "Now SEQ Running"  /*0x10*/,
      "Song Data Exist"  /*0x11*/, "Internal Bat.NG"  /*0x12*/,
      "Data Card Bat.NG" /*0x13*/, "ID Mismatch"      /*0x14*/,
      "No Wave Card"     /*0x15*/, "Wrong Wave Card"  /*0x16*/,
      "Now SEQ Runnning" /*0x17*/, "(not defined)"    /*0x18*/,
      "Voice Type"       /*0x19*/, "Song cleared"     /*0x1A*/,
      "(not error 0x1B)" /*0x1B*/, "(not error 0x1C)" /*0x1C*/,
      "(not error 0x1D)" /*0x1D*/, "Bulk Received"    /*0x1E*/,
      "Bulk Receiving"   /*0x1F*/, "Bulk Canceled"    /*0x20*/
    };
    pi = new SyxParamInfo(prt,'e',UID_ERROR_MSG,"Error Message");
    pi.setValueMap(errVals,errMsgs);
    prt.addParamInfo(pi);

      Now we can parse the received SysEx message:

          byte[] syxData = sxMsg.getMessage();
    SyxMessage errMsg = new SyxMessage(prt);
    errMsg.setMessage(syxData,syxData.length);

      Let's look what we've got. We print different views on the parsed SysEx message. First the message itself:

          System.out.println("Received error message from TG55:");
    System.out.println(errMsg.prettyPrint());
 
    > Received error message from TG55:
    > 0000 F0H (System exclusive)
    > Part   0 : 0001 'Error Information' (9 bytes) 
    > 0001 43H - 01000011 11111111
    > 0002 10H - 0001#### 11110000 : # ( 4 bits) =   0x00     0      Device = 1
    > 0003 35H - 00110101 11111111
    > 0004 7FH - 01111111 11111111
    > 0005 00H - 00000000 11111111
    > 0006 00H - 00000000 11111111
    > 0007 00H - 00000000 11111111
    > 0008 00H - 00000000 11111111
    > 0009 20H - 0eeeeeee 10000000 : e ( 7 bits) =   0x20    32  ' ' Error Message = Bulk Canceled
    > EOX
    > 0010 F7H (System Common - End of system exclusive)
    > 11 bytes

      Then we print details of the data part:

          System.out.println("\nDetails of Part 0: "+prt.prettyPrint());
    
    > Details of Part 0: 'Error Information'
    > - Length: 9 bytes
    > - Data:   43 10 35 7F 00 00 00 00 20 
    >           01000011 00010000 00110101 01111111 00000000 00000000 00000000 00000000 
    >           00100000 
    > - Format: 01000011 0001#### 00110101 01111111 00000000 00000000 00000000 00000000
    >           0eeeeeee 
    > - Filter: 11111111 11110000 11111111 11111111 11111111 11111111 11111111 11111111 
    >           10000000 
    > - Params: # - length  : 4 bits
    >               value   : 0x00  0
    >               default : 0x00  0
    >               UID     : 'DEVICE_NUM'
    >               descr   : 'Device'
    >               par.chg.: (not assigned)
    >               val.rng.: [0,15]
    >               val.ofs.: 1
    >           e - length  : 7 bits
    >               value   : 0x20  32  ' ' = 'Bulk Canceled'
    >               default : 0x01  1
    >               UID     : 'ERROR_MSG'
    >               descr   : 'Error Message'
    >               par.chg.: (not assigned)
    >               values  : 0x01: 'MIDI Buffer Full'
    >                         0x02: 'SEQ Buffer Full'
    >                         0x03: 'MIDI Data'
    >                         0x04: 'MIDI Check Sum'
    >                         0x05: 'MIDI Device Off'
    >                         0x06: 'MIDI Bulk Prot.'
    >                         0x07: 'No Data Card'
    >                         0x08: 'Data Card Prot.'
    >                         0x09: 'Data Format'
    >                         0x0A: 'Illegal Data'
    >                         0x0B: 'Verify Failed'
    >                         0x0C: 'Internal Bat.Lo'
    >                         0x0D: 'Data Card Bat.Lo'
    >                         0x0E: 'SEQ Memory Full'
    >                         0x0F: 'SEQ Data Empty'
    >                         0x10: 'Now SEQ Running'
    >                         0x11: 'Song Data Exist'
    >                         0x12: 'Internal Bat.NG'
    >                         0x13: 'Data Card Bat.NG'
    >                         0x14: 'ID Mismatch'
    >                         0x15: 'No Wave Card'
    >                         0x16: 'Wrong Wave Card'
    >                         0x17: 'Now SEQ Runnning'
    >                         0x18: '(not defined)'
    >                         0x19: 'Voice Type'
    >                         0x1A: 'Song cleared'
    >                         0x1B: '(not error 0x1B)'
    >                         0x1C: '(not error 0x1C)'
    >                         0x1D: '(not error 0x1D)'
    >                         0x1E: 'Bulk Received'
    >                         0x1F: 'Bulk Receiving'
    >                         0x20: 'Bulk Canceled'

      Finally, we print the message properties along with their values:

          System.out.println("Message properties:");
    String UIDs[] = errMsg.findParamUIDsSimple("*");
    for (String UID : UIDs)
    {
      String mVal = errMsg.getModelValueAsString(UID);
      String s = String.format("%-10s = %s",UID,mVal);
      System.out.println(s);
    }
    
    > Message properties:
    > DEVICE_NUM = 1
    > ERROR_MSG  = Bulk Canceled
    
  }

    • fetchPatchName

      public static void fetchPatchName()
      Demonstrates how to fetch a patch name from a device.

      [<prev] | Back to [Advanced Examples] [Tutorials] | [next>]

      Fetching a Patch Name

      We will (synchronously) fetch a patch name from a Yamaha TG55. You may rewrite the example for your own device as an exercise. The device must support patch bulk requests.

      We create, open and close the MIDI interface as explained in Getting Started/Creating a MIDI Interface.

        1  final int     devNum = 0; // <- Your zero-based device number here
  2  final String  miName = "KOMPLETE KONTROL EXT - 1"; // <- Name of your MIDI interface here
  3  MidiInterface mi     = null;
  4 
  5  try
  6  {
  7    mi = new MidiInterface(miName);
  8    mi.open();

      The first step is to create the TG55 voice bulk dump message we want to receive (see [1], pp. Add-6, 9-11). Fetching the voice bulk will be synchronized on this object.

        9    final SyxMessage blkDmp = new SyxMessage();

      Next we register a SysEx message listener with the MIDI interface:

       10    mi.addSysExListener(new ISysexMessageListener()
 11    {
 12 
 13      @Override
 14      public void receiveSysexMsg(SysexMessage sxMsg, long timeStamp)
 15      throws InvalidMidiDataException
 16      {

      Upon receiving a SysEx message, we first just print the raw data:

       17        rcvrLog.log(0,2,"SyxEx message received:\n");
 18        byte[] syxData = sxMsg.getMessage();
 19        rcvrLog.log(SYX.prettyPrintByteArray(syxData,""));
 20        rcvrLog.log(0,-2,"\n\n");

      (rcvrLog is an interface to SYX's global message logger supplied by class AdvancedExamples to ease console print-outs).

      Now we detect whether the received SysEx message is a TG55 bulk dump (of any type). To this end, we initially only parse the message header. If this fails, an InvalidMidiDataException will be thrown indicating to the SysEx message listener framework that our receiver does not understand and process the received message. This preliminary step is necessary because TG55 voice bulk messages, which we want to receive, contain a variable number of payload bytes depending on the voice settings. We need the payload length in preparation for parsing the voice bulk.

       21        try
 22        {
 23          SyxMessage rcvMsg = new SyxMessage();
 24          rcvMsg.addParts(new SyxDataStruct(

      
Ungültige Referenz

      TG55
      

      
.

      
Ungültige Referenz

      F_BD_HEADER1
      

      
,"Bulk Header 1"));
 25          rcvMsg.setMessage(sxMsg.getMessage(),rcvMsg.getLength()/*Header bytes only!*/);
 26          rcvrLog.log(0,2,"TG55 bulk header 1 parsed successfully:\n");
 27          rcvrLog.log(rcvMsg.prettyPrint());
 28          rcvrLog.log(0,-2,"\n\n");

      The payload size is contained in parameter 'b' of the bulk header (see

      Ungültige Referenz 
      format specification
      and [1] p. Add-6):

       29          int bc = rcvMsg.getPart(0).getMidiValue('b');

      Now we're all set to actually parse the received voice bulk:

       30          rcvMsg.clear();
 31          rcvMsg.addParts(new SyxDataStruct(

      
Ungültige Referenz

      TG55
      

      
.

      
Ungültige Referenz

      F_BD_HEADER1
      

      
 ,"Bulk Header 1"));
 32          rcvMsg.addParts(new SyxDataStruct(

      
Ungültige Referenz

      TG55
      

      
.

      
Ungültige Referenz

      F_BD_HEADER2
      

      
 ,"Bulk Header 2"));
 33          rcvMsg.addParts(new SyxDataStruct(

      
Ungültige Referenz

      TG55
      

      
.

      
Ungültige Referenz

      F_BDVC_HEADER
      

      
,"Voice Header" ));
 34          bc -= (rcvMsg.getPart(1).getLength() + rcvMsg.getPart(2).getLength());
 35          rcvMsg.addParts(new SyxDataStruct("**H ".repeat(bc) ,"(Other Data)"));
 36          rcvMsg.addParts(new SyxChecksum(SyxChecksum.ROLAND,1,-1));
 37          rcvMsg.setMessage(sxMsg.getMessage(),rcvMsg.getLength());

      In lines 31–36 we have created the entire voice bulk message structure. The part added in line 35 is a dummy just accepting all bytes of the voice bulk except the voice header (see line 33 and [1] p. Add-9) which contains the patch name. Only the size of the dummy part must be correct. Hence, we used the playload size obtained in line 29 to make its format specifier (lines 34, 35). In line 37 we parsed the received message. If this fails, an InvalidMidiDataException will be thrown, again indicating to the framework that our receiver does not understand and process this message.

      So far, so good. In a last step, we have to check the device number, to make sure we got the correct bulk type, and to validate the checksum: 

       38          int dn = rcvMsg.getPart(0).getMidiValue('#');
 39          if (dn!=devNum)
 40            throw SYX.InvMdataExc("Wrong devive no. %d; must be %d",dn,devNum);
 41          String bid = rcvMsg.getPart(1).readString("st");
 42          if (!"VC".equals(bid))
 43            throw SYX.InvMdataExc("Bad bulk ID '%s'; must be 'VC'",bid);
 44          rcvMsg.validateChecksum();
 45          rcvrLog.log("TG55 voice bulk dump parsed successfully\n");

      Finally, we synchronize on blkDmp, copy the parsed message into it, and notify the main thread of having successfully received a TG55 voice bulk:

       46          rcvrLog.log("-> Notifying MAIN\n\n");
 47          synchronized (blkDmp)
 48          {
 49            blkDmp.copy(rcvMsg);
 50            blkDmp.notifyAll();
 51          }
 52        }

      The try-catch block starting in line 21 is not essential to the function. It was just inserted to print any InvalidMidiDataExceptions that might have occurred. After that, we just re-throw the exception.

       53        catch (InvalidMidiDataException e)
 54        {
 55          rcvrLog.errlog("Not a TG55 voice bulk (%s)",e.getMessage());
 56          throw e;
 57        }
 58      }
 59
 60      @Override
 61      public int getDevNum()
 62      {
 63        return devNum;
 64      }
 65
 66    });

      Back in the main thread, we create a TG55 voice bulk request message: 

       67    SyxDataStruct prt = new SyxDataStruct(

      
Ungültige Referenz

      TG55
      

      
.

      
Ungültige Referenz

      F_BULK_REQUEST
      

      
);
 68    prt.setMidiValue('#',devNum); // Device no.
 69    prt.setMidiValue('s','V'   ); // Voice bulk type
 70    prt.setMidiValue('t','C'   ); // Voice bulk type
 71    prt.setMidiValue('x',0x00  ); // Patch I01 (Bank no.)
 72    prt.setMidiValue('y',0x00  ); // Patch I01 (Patch no.)
 73    SyxMessage blkReq = new SyxMessage(prt);

      Then we clear the expected voice bulk dump, synchronize on it, send the bulk request, and wait for the bulk dump to arrive:

       74    long then = System.nanoTime();
 75    blkDmp.clear();
 76    synchronized (blkDmp)
 77    {
 78      mainLog.log("Requesting TG55 voice bulk dump (Patch I01)\n");
 79      mi.send(blkReq);
 80      try
 81      {
 82        mainLog.log("Waiting for SYX_RCVR\n\n");
 83        blkDmp.wait(2000);
 84      }
 85      catch (InterruptedException e)
 86      { // Ignore
 87      }
 88    }

      (mainLog is an interface to SYX's global message logger supplied by class AdvancedExamples to ease console print-outs).

      If we received a voice bulk dump in reply to the request, blkDmp will be non-empty. In this case, we read-out and print the patch name. If blkDmp is empty, the request timed out.

       89    if (!blkDmp.isEmpty())
 90    {
 91      SyxDataStruct vceHdr = blkDmp.getPart(2);
 92      String patchName = vceHdr.readString("a-j");
 93      mainLog.log("Patch I01 - '%s'\n",patchName);
 94      long now = System.nanoTime();
 95      mainLog.log("(Turnaround time: %.0f ms)\n",(now-then)/1000000.);
 96    }
 97    else
 98      mainLog.errlog("TIME OUT!\n");
 99   }
100  catch (Exception e)
101  {
102    e.printStackTrace();
103  }
104  finally
105  {
106    if (mi!=null)
107      mi.close();
108  }

      References:

      [1] Yamaha Corp.: TG55 Operating Manual. Online, retrieved May 12, 2026

    • syxFiles

      public static void syxFiles()
      Demonstrates reading and writing SYX files.

      [<prev] | Back to [Advanced Examples] [Tutorials] | [next>]

      Reading and Writing SysEx Files

      SYX provides a SysEx recoder which contains a "tape" whose "tracks" are SysEx messages. The tape can be recorded, played back, and written to or read from a SYX or TXT file.

      Note: This example requires the SYX.Devices package!

      Let's record a little SysEx tape:

       try
 {
   SysexRecorder recorder = new SysexRecorder();
   recorder.record(

      
Ungültige Referenz

      VZ1PatchAppc
      

      
.

      
Ungültige Referenz

      vz1Patch_Karamoon
      

      
());
   recorder.record(

      
Ungültige Referenz

      VZ1PatchAppc
      

      
.

      
Ungültige Referenz

      vz1Patch_NothingToFear
      

      
());

      The tape now contains two demo patches of the Casio VZ-1/10M synthesizer.

      We save the patches into a temporary SYX file:

         File tmpFile = File.createTempFile("syxtape",".syx");
   tmpFile.deleteOnExit();
   String fileName = tmpFile.getAbsolutePath();
   recorder.writeSyxFile(fileName);

      Then we clear the tape and reload it from the SYX file:

         recorder.reset();
   recorder.readSyxFile(fileName);

      Now we play the reloaded tape back to a SysEx message listener which we attach to the recorder:

         recorder.addSysExListener(new ISysexMessageListener()
   {

     @Override
     public void receiveSysexMsg(SysexMessage sxMsg, long timeStamp)
     throws InvalidMidiDataException
     {
       

      
Ungültige Referenz

      VZ1Patch
      

      
 vz1Patch = new 

      
Ungültige Referenz

      VZ1Patch
      

      
(sxMsg.getData());
       System.out.println(vz1Patch.

      
Ungültige Referenz

      prettyPrintModel
      

      
());
     }

     @Override
     public int getDevNum()
     { // Not needed
       return 0;
     }

   });
   recorder.play();
 }
 catch (Exception e)
 {
   e.printStackTrace();
 }

    • main

      public static void main(String[] args)
